coredata.dc:description: Widespread bacterial infection and the spread of antibiotic resistance exhibit increasing threat to the public and thus require new antibacterial strategies. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been extensively investigated to play fluorescent, catalytic roles and even potential biomedical functions containing sterilization. However, synthetic understanding of the interaction of CQDs and bacteria, the exhibition of antibacterial ability, and the risk of resistance evolution remain lacking. Herein, a simple one-pot method was fabricated to prepare positively charged CQDs (PC-CQDs) as a broad-spectrum antibacterial agent. PC-CQDs possessed effective antibacterial activity against all tested Gram-positive, Gram-negative, and drug-resistant bacteria. Investigation of the antibacterial mechanism of PC-CQDs indicated that small-sized PC-CQDs functionalized with –NH2 and –NH induced strong adherence behavior on the bacterial cell membrane. Moreover, the entry of PC-CQDs caused conformational changes in the genes and generation of reactive oxygen species in the bacteria. Safety evaluation illustrated that PC-CQDs did not trigger detectable drug resistance or hemolysis. Furthermore, PC-CQDs effectively promoted the antibacterial treatment of mixed Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli infected wound in rats with low in vivo toxicity. These results suggested that PC-CQDs are a potential antibacterial candidate for real wound healing applications in complex bacterial infections and even resistant bacteria-caused infections.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of PC-CQDs. (A) Scheme of the preparation of PC-CQDs. (B) TEM image and (C) FTIR spectrum of PC-CQDs. (D–G) Full-range XPS spectrum of PC-CQDs and high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s. (H) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of PC-CQDs. Inset in (A) is the size distribution and HRTEM of PC-CQDs. Inset in (H) is an image of PC-CQDs under light and 365 nm excitation.
(A) Bactericidal curve and (B) bacteriostatic curve of different concentrations of PC-CQDs against S. aureus, MRSA, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa at different periods.

(A) Zeta-potential of PC-CQDs, NC-CQDs, S. aureus, the interaction system of PC-CQDs and S. aureus, the interaction system of NC-CQDs and S. aureus, E. coli, the interaction system of PC-CQDs and E. coli, and the interaction system of NC-CQDs and E. coli. (B) CLSM of the interaction system of S. aureus and PC-CQDs or NC-CQDs. (C) the schematic diagram of the cell wall structure of S. aureus and E. coli. (D) CLSM of the interaction system of PC-CQDs and S. aureus or E. coli.
DAPI/PI staining of S. aureus, S. aureus with 200 μg/mL PC-CQDs, E. coli and E. coli with 500 μg/mL PC-CQDs using CLSM.
TEM (A) and SEM (B) images of S. aureus (a and d), E. coli (b and e), and MRSA (c and f) without treatment (a, b and c) or with treatment of PC-CQDs (d, e and f).

Resistance acquisition of S. aureus (A), E. coli (B), and MRSA (C) during continuous exposure to PC-CQDs. (D) Cell viability of MC3T3-E1 cells to different contents of PC-CQDs at 12 or 24 h of culture. Images of hemolysis (E) and hemolysis ratio (F) of different concentrations of PC-CQDs on red blood cell. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Schematic illustration of in vivo process of infected wound model with mixture of S. aureus and E. coli; wound pictures(B) and wound areas (C) of the healing process of the infected wounds in the control group (control, normal saline), experimental group (PC-CQDs), and positive group (levofloxacin) at different periods. The VIII line in (B) is the amplification of VII line.

WBC counts (A) and CRP levels (B) of the healing process of the infected wounds in the control group (control, normal saline), experimental group (PC-CQDs), and positive group (levofloxacin) at different periods. (C) Colonies of bacterial culture of the wound tissue after total treatment of infected wounds. (D) H&E staining (×100) of the wound tissues before and after treatment in different groups.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Widespread bacterial infection and the spread of antibiotic resistance exhibit increasing threat to the public and thus require new antibacterial strategies. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been extensively investigated to play fluorescent, catalytic roles and even potential biomedical functions containing sterilization. However, synthetic understanding of the interaction of CQDs and bacteria, the exhibition of antibacterial ability, and the risk of resistance evolution remain lacking. Herein, a simple one-pot method was fabricated to prepare positively charged CQDs (PC-CQDs) as a broad-spectrum antibacterial agent. PC-CQDs possessed effective antibacterial activity against all tested Gram-positive, Gram-negative, and drug-resistant bacteria. Investigation of the antibacterial mechanism of PC-CQDs indicated that small-sized PC-CQDs functionalized with –NH2 and –NH induced strong adherence behavior on the bacterial cell membrane. Moreover, the entry of PC-CQDs caused conformational changes in the genes and generation of reactive oxygen species in the bacteria. Safety evaluation illustrated that PC-CQDs did not trigger detectable drug resistance or hemolysis. Furthermore, PC-CQDs effectively promoted the antibacterial treatment of mixed Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli infected wound in rats with low in vivo toxicity. These results suggested that PC-CQDs are a potential antibacterial candidate for real wound healing applications in complex bacterial infections and even resistant bacteria-caused infections.

ce:section.ce:para: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) causes increased threat in clinic and public [1,2]. A report from WHO about global antibiotic resistance showed that increased bacteria have developed resistance to existing drugs, and the infection of drug-resistant bacteria in 2020 has increased by approximately 9% compared with that in 2019 [3]. Severe AMR has led to the need to the rational administration of drugs and to explore antimicrobial agents that kill drug-resistant bacteria and with the capacities of preventing the evolution of AMR or further AMR process [4,5]. New kinds of antibiotics from synthetic chemistry [6], multichannel device (iChip) [7], and artificial intelligence [8] have been developed for effective therapies that combat bacteria and even drug-resistant bacteria [9]. However, a breakthrough in antibiotic development remains lacking due to the potential toxicity, the decline in investment, and the risk of drug resistance [10]. Therefore, the task to explore other effective antibacterial agents that has a different mode of action compared with traditional antibiotics is challenging but attractive. Antimicrobial peptides [11,12], bacteriophages [13], probiotics [14], and nanomaterials [15,16] were designed and underwent extended trials for combating or reducing serious bacterial infections. Fabricating effective antibacterial agents that are neither damaged by the existing AMR mechanism nor the cause of the evolution of further AMR is highly important.

Compared with antibiotics mainly derived from microorganisms or natural products [17,18], some nanoparticles (NPs) exhibit superior performance in antibacterial aspects due to the development of nanoscience and technology [15,19–23]. For example, NPs have the potential for multiple antibacterial effects with enhanced bactericidal efficiency ascribed to the advantage of the enhanced interaction and permeability of NPs to bacterial cell [24,25]. Nanotechnology could reasonably design the structure [26–28] and activity of NPs [23,29] to show potentially enhanced bactericidal ability [30–33] with reduced biotoxicity. However, some antibacterial nanomaterials could also cause bacterial drug resistance [34,35], which motivates the further search of effective antibacterial nanomaterials without inducible drug resistance on the basis of clear antibacterial action mode.

Among various antibacterial nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted increased attention from the optical field [36–39] to antibacterial activity [40–42] due to their biocompatibility, ease of preparation, modulated structure, and functional properties [22,43,44]. In the research of antibacterial CQDs, generating strong interactions between bacterial cells and CQDs caused by the design of structures or surface molecules is also a crucial factor. Compared with the reported antibacterial CQDs, those with positive surface through the modification of a specific group or macromolecule are the main objective antibacterial nanomaterials that could generate strong antibacterial capacity on bacteria for improved drug concentration in the target. Moreover, the bactericidal mechanism of CQDs is based either on the destructive effect of CQDs on gene [45] or on the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS)-related oxidative stress with the assistance of introduced H2O2 [46,47] or light irradiation [48–50]. Systematic investigation about the interaction, location, and combating of CQDs on bacteria and evaluation of possible further AMR evolution in antibacterial CQDs are still lacking. Thus, understanding the interaction and antibacterial mechanism of CQDs with broad-spectrum antibacterial abilities and decreased minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) but without inducible resistance is a challenging and necessary task.

CQDs could generally achieve high fluorescence performance and adjustable optical application via heteroatom (like nitrogen [36–39]) doping or surface modification [51–54], and these practices could be expected to enrich the action of CQDs on bacteria for enhanced antibacterial activity. In the present work, considering the negative surface charge of bacteria and by using citric acid as carbon source, positively charged nitrogen-doped CQDs (PC-CQDs) were designed and prepared using a simple strategy to improve the adhesive ability on bacteria. The antibacterial properties of PC-CQDs against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria were evaluated. Systematical investigation of the antibacterial mechanism illustrated that PC-CQDs acted on bacteria through electrostatic enrichment and then interfered with bacteria on gene and produced ROS. Such action routine caused the rupture of bacteria. Drug resistance induction experiments found that PC-CQDs did not induce resistance evolution over 30 days of interaction with Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Escherichia coli (E. coli), and methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA). The in vivo experimental results showed that PC-CQDs and levofloxacin hydrochloride had the same therapeutic effect on the wound model of S. aureus and E. coli mixed infection.

Materials, apparatus, Sterilization and Bacteriostatic curve of PC-CQDs, the staining of Live/dead bacteria, transmission electron microscope (TEM) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) characterization of PC-CQDs against bacteria, CLSM characterization for the evaluation of ROS, H&E staining, and Circular dichroism (CD) measurement were illustrated in the supplementary materials.

Citric acid (3.0 g) and l-glutathione (1.0 g, GSH) were fully mixed in a 25 mL round bottom flask. The mixture was heated at 140 °C for 30 min to obtain a melted liquid. After the heating temperature was increased from 140 °Cto 180 °C, 10 mL of polyethene polyamine (PEPA) was added into the melted liquid. After continuous heating for 1 h at 180 °C, the mixture was cooled down. Acetone was added and the product was centrifugally separated. Then, the precipitate was purified through dialysis. After freeze-drying was conducted, solid PC-CQDs were obtained and stored at 4 °C in the dark. In accordance with the same procedure of PC-NCDs but without the introduction of PEPA, negatively charged CQDs (NC-CQDs) were also prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial culture and preparation of bacterial suspension
ce:section.ce:para: All testing bacteria were first inoculated on blood agar plates via streak plate method and cultured in a biochemical incubator at 35 °C to obtain single colony. A single colony was selected by using an inoculation ring and inoculated in sterile normal saline to obtain the bacterial suspension. The optical density at 600 nm (OD600) was measured and adjusted to 0.1 to obtain 1.5 × 108 CFU/mL of bacterial suspension. The bacterial suspension with the OD600 of 0.1 could be, which was directly applied or diluted for the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity test of PC-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Disk diffusion test: by using sterile medical swabs, the 1.5 × 108 CFU/mL bacterial suspension was smeared evenly on the MH agar medium (Φ = 90 mm), and the disks containing a certain amount of PC-CQDs were placed onto the above medium. The center space of each disk was at least 24 mm. After the disks were incubated in a biochemical incubator for 18 h at 35 °C the diameter of the inhibition zone around each disk was measured and recorded with vernier caliper. Each disk diffusion test was carried out three times independently.

Constant broth dilution test: 13 sterile test tubes (12 × 75 mm) were placed in a row and numbered successively. Except for tube No. 1, each tube was added with 1.0 mL MH broth. After 1 mL of PC-CQDs solution with a concentration of 3.84 mg/mL was added into tubes No. 1 and No. 2, the 1 mL of mixture in tube No. 2 was taken out and added into tube No. 3. After mixing, 1 mL of mixture in tube No. 3 was taken out and added into tube No. 4, and the operation was repeated until tube No. 11. The 1 mL mixture in tube No. 11 was taken out and discarded, and tube No. 12 was used as the growth control that did not contain PC-CQDs. The concentrations of PC-CQDs in each tube (Nos. 1–11) were 3840, 1920, 960, 480, 240, 120, 60, 30, 15, 7.5, and 3.75 μg/mL, respectively. Then, 1 mL of bacterial suspension (1.5 × 106 CFU/mL) was added into tube Nos. 1–12 to obtain a bacterial concentration of 7.5 × 105 CFU/mL in each tube; the concentrations were 1920, 960, 480, 240, 120, 60, 30, 15, 7.5, 3.75, and 1.875 μg/mL, respectively. After the above test tubes were placed in biochemical incubator and incubated for 16–20 h at 35 °C, the lowest concentration of the bacteria-free tube was the MIC of the PC-CQDs for the tested bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessments of AMR evolution in bacteria after long-term exposure to PC-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the obtained MIC of PC-CQDs from the test tube dilution method, AMR evolution tests were performed in accordance with the reported methods [4,5,55] with minor revision to assess the impact of PC-CQDs on S. aureus, E. coli, and MRSA by sequential culture of bacteria at sub-MIC. First, 7.5 × 105 CFU/mL bacteria were inoculated into MH broth containing dosages of 2 × MIC, 4 × MIC, 8 × MIC, and 16 × MIC PC-CQDs. Next, after the broth was incubated for 24 h at 37 °C, it was spread on a nutrient plate without antibacterial materials to evaluate the sterilization effect. Then, S. aureus, E. coli, and MRSA were grown in culture broth containing 2 mL of PC-CQDs with a gradient increment of 0.25 × MIC, 0.5 × MIC, 1 × MIC, 2 × MIC, and 4 × MIC concentrations. Culture growth was checked by spreading the broth on drug-free nutrient agar every 24 h. Then, the colonies with a certain inhibitory effect on the growth but could ensure the basic growth of the bacteria were selected to prepare a bacterial suspension by using physiological saline. Subsequently, the bacterial saline suspension was added to the fresh medium containing 0.25 × MIC, 0.5 × MIC, 1 × MIC, 2 × MIC, and 4 × MIC concentrations of PC-CQDs on the basis of the above repeated steps. This continuous passage was repeated every day for 30 days. The experiment was repeated and produced the same negative result.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity test of PC-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: MC3T3-E1 cells were cultured in MEM medium containing 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum and 1% (v/v) penicillin at 37°Cin 5% CO2. The cells were plated in 96-well plates and cultured for 24 h, and then the original medium was replaced with MEM medium containing gradient concentrations of PC-CQDs (15.625, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, and 500 μg/mL). After 12 or 24 h of culture with PC-CQDs, a medium containing 10% CCK-8 was added and cultured for 2 h. The absorbance in each well at 450 nm was measured and compared.

With the consent of healthy volunteers, human blood samples were obtained from the First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University. First, red blood cells were collected through standard centrifugation procedure. Then, they were dispersed in 1 mL of normal saline. Afterwards, the cells were centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 15 min. Then, 50 μL of erythrocyte suspension was dispersed in 1 mL of normal saline containing PC-CQDs, mixed, and incubated at 37°Cfor 3 h. Finally, the erythrocyte suspension was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min. The absorbance of the supernatant at a wavelength of 540 nm was measured, and the hemolysis rate was calculated. Normal saline and sterilized water were used as negative control andpositive control, respectively. The operation was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antibacterial activity of PC-CQDs towards mixed bacteria infection
ce:section.ce:para: The animal experiment was approved by the Experimental Animal Ethics Committee of Fujian Medical University, and the operation was carried out in accordance with the “Animal Experiment Guide” (No. FJMU IACUC2018-083). All clean Sprague–Dawley rats in the experiment were purchased from the Experimental Animal Center of Fujian Medical University.

A total of 21 rats weighing 279 ± 10 g were randomly divided into three groups: negative group (treated with saline, nine rats), experimental group (treated with PC-CQDs, six rats), and positive group (treated with levofloxacin hydrochloride, six rats). After the rats were anesthetized with 10% chloral hydrate solution (applying 0.3 mL/100 g) by intraperitoneal injection, the entire layer of the skin was excised with a scalpel to form a round wound with a diameter of approximately 20 mm [56,57]. Then, a patch containing 2 mL of mixed bacterial suspension (S. aureus and E. coli at a concentration of 3.0 × 108 CFU/mL) was adhered to the wound surface and fixed with a mesh elastic bandage for 48 h. The presence of obvious pustules on the wound surface confirmed the successful wound infection.

Three infected rats in the negative group were euthanized, and the infected wound tissue was dissected and used for pathological analysis. The six rats in the negative group (control) was treated with a dressing containing 2 mL of sterile saline. The experimental group (PC-CQDs)was treated with a patch containing 2 mL of 5 mg/mL PC-CQDs. The positive group (levofloxacin hydrochloride) was treated with a patch containing 2 mL of 2 mg/mL levofloxacin hydrochloride. During the treatment for 14 days, the interventional patch used in each group was changed once a day, and the venous blood of each group of rats was collected at the legal point of tail blood collection. On the 14th day, all the rats were sacrificed, and the wound tissues were harvested. The obtained tissues were homogenized in sterile normal saline to obtain homogenized tissues, and the colonies of the residual bacteria in the tissues from the three groups were cultured and compared.

In the experimental process, the venous blood of each group was collected via tail-point blood collection for routine blood test to evaluate the healing process. Photos of the wounds in each group were recorded.

All data were expressed in mean standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis was performed on IBM SPSS Statistics 23 statistical software package. *p < 0.05 and **p < 0.01 indicated that the difference was statistically significant.

In a typical synthesis, PC-CQDs were prepared via melting process under stage heating (Fig. 1 A). TEM was used to study the morphology of PC-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1B, TEM results revealed that PC-CQDs were quasi-spherical with good dispersion and uniformity. The size was relatively regular, and the distribution was narrow, with a mean size of 2.5 nm. High-resolution TEM showed that the lattice spacing of PC-CQDs was 0.24 nm, which was attributed to the (100) diffraction of carbon nanomaterials [58]. Surface groups are important factors that affect the properties and adhesive ability of CQDs [59]; thus, FTIR and XPS were applied to investigate the molecular structure of PC-CQDs. In Fig. 1C, the FTIR absorption peak at 1649 cm−1 was due to the specific stretching vibration of CO in PC-CQDs [60,61]. The two peaks at 1565 and 1124 cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibration of NH and the stretching vibration of C–N–C, respectively [62,63]. Furthermore, the broad bands at 3420 and 3272 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibration absorption of –OH and –NH2. FTIR confirmed the doping effect of N and the presence of the abundance of hydrophilic groups of–OH and –NH2 in PC-CQDs. Moreover, zeta-potential measurement of the PC-CQDs in water exhibited a value of +12.9 mV, suggesting the positively charged surface caused by –NH2 or –NH, which was also confirmed by XPS (Fig. 1D). In the full-scan XPS spectrum, the element peaks of C1s, N1s, O1s, and S 2p were identified, with atomic contents of 73.65%, 15.66%, 10.59%, and 0.11%, respectively. The presence of a small amount of S may be ascribed to the application of GSH as reacting substance. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) was deconvoluted into four binding energy peaks of 284.3, 284.9, 285.7, and 287.3 eV, corresponding to CC, CC, C–O/C–N, and C=O/C=N [59,61,63], respectively. Similarly, the N1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) consisted two individual fitting peaks at 398.7 and 399.4 eV, which were assigned to C–NH–C and –NH2, respectively [59]. Furthermore, the O1s spectrum (Fig. 1G) was devoted to two fitting peaks of 530.9 and 532.5 eV that could be assigned to C–O–C/C–OH and O–C=O [64], respectively. The results of XPS analysis was consistent with the FTIR result in confirming the dual function of N as a doping effect and as a surface functional group. Moreover, the PC-CQDs exhibited an broad absorption peak at 345 nm and a band absorption peak at 212 nm in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 1H), representing the n-π* transition of CO and the π-π* transition of CC in the aromatic ring structure of PC-CQDs, respectively [64,65]. The PC-CQDs demonstrated multiple emission spectra with a slight peak shift from 425 nm to 460 nm when excited from 310 nm to 360 nm (Fig. S1). The PC-CQDs also obtained the maximum fluorescence emission centered at 430 nm with exciting at 350 nm (Fig. 1H). Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the quantum yield of the PC-CQDs was 15.24% (Fig. S2), suggesting considerable luminescence properties [60,66,67]. Meanwhile, a yellow picture under sunlight and a bright blue fluorescence image under 365 nm of UV light were shown. Furthermore, the fluorescence ability of PC-CQDs ensure it as an effective probe for cell imaging. Fig. S3 showed that MC3T3-E1 cells can be clear stained blue with the illustration of Cellular pseudopodia by PC-CQDs under 405 nm exciting. These results indicated that the PC-CQDs conformed to the morphology, molecular structure, and luminescent properties of CQDs with the essence of the carbon core and the modification of characteristic functional groups [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity of PC-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The MIC of PC-CQDs against selected bacteria, such as S. aureus, MRSA, L. monocytogenes, E. faecalis, E. coli, S. marcescens, P. aeruginosa, drug-resistant E. coli, and drug-resistant P. aeruginosa isolated from the clinic were determined using constant broth dilution test, as shown in Table S1. The PC-CQDs exhibited a certain antibacterial effect on Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria and better antibacterial effect with smaller MIC on Gram-positive bacteria than on Gram-negative bacteria. The PC-CQDs also exhibited the best antibacterial effect on S. aureus, with the smallest MIC of 15 μg/mL. Furthermore, they demonstrated antibacterial activity on resistant bacteria. The MIC for MRSA was 30 μg/mL, much better than that for other nitrogen-doped CQDs [57]. For the multi-drug resistant E. coli and P. aeruginosa isolated from the clinic, the PC-CQDs exhibited antibacterial ability with MIC of 480 μg/mL. Disk diffusion test was further performed to evaluate the antibacterial effect of PC-CQDs to bacterial growth [57]. As shown in Fig. S4, the disks on each MH agar plate contained PC-CQDs with contents of 1 mL × MIC, 2 mL × MIC, and 4 mL × MIC. Using S. aureus, E. coli, and MRSA as the representative strain, all of the disks containing PC-CQDs showed bacteriostatic circles even on the concentration of MIC, while the discs containing 4 mL × MIC of PC-CQDs could form obvious bacteriostatic circles with a diameter higher than 12 mm.

The bactericidal and bacteriostatic curves of PC-CQDs against the selected four bacteria of S. aureus, MRSA, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa were evaluated. The number of colonies of the four different bacteria on agar medium at different periods under different concentrations of PC-CQDs were determined. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the PC-CQDs with four gradients were set to investigate the combating effect of PC-CQDs on variable bacteria. For the blank control groups, all of the four bacteria grew rapidly after 6–8 h and continued to increase to 12 h without the addition of PC-CQDs. When the dosage of PC-CQDs was at MIC, the 4-h sterilization rates of S. aureus, MRSA, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa reached 99.21%, 99.92%, 93.68%, and 92.09%, respectively (Fig. S5). In addition, 2 × MIC PC-CQDs could almost kill S. aureus and MRSA within 8 h. Correspondingly, E. coli and P. aeruginosa showed a persistent downward trend within 12 h with the dosages of PC-CQDs at MIC and 2 × MIC. The sterilization rates of E. coli and P. aeruginosa reached 98.42% and 94.47%, respectively, in 4 h at 2 × MIC PC-CQDs. The PC-CQDs at 4 × MIC could completely kill S. aureus and MRSA at 6 h and P. aeruginosa at 12 h (Fig. 2A), while PC-CQDs at 8 × MIC could completely kill S. aureus and MRSA within 4 h and completely kill E. coli and P. aeruginosa at 8 h. The time-dependent bacteriostatic curves of PC-CQDs at 1/16 × MIC, 1/8 × MIC, 1/4 × MIC, 1/2 × MIC, and MIC on the bacteria were evaluated using OD600 measurement (Fig. 2B). The OD600 values of the four selected bacterial broths were very small at the initial 6 h with or without the addition of PC-CQDs, indicating slow bacterial growth before 6 h. After >6 h, the OD600 value increased rapidly in an exponential form without the dosage of PC-CQDs. Correspondingly, the values were obviously lower in the bacteria with PC-CQDs at different contents, suggesting the inhibitory effect of PC-CQDs on the selected bacterial species. The different OD600 values caused by the introduced PC-CQDs on the different bacteria indicated the different inhibition degrees of PC-CQDs. Furthermore, the OD600 value decreased with the increase in the concentration of PC-CQDs, indicating that PC-CQDs were bacteriostatic in a concentration-dependent manner. Compared with the group without PC-CQDs, the PC-CQDs at 1/2 × MIC had a significant inhibitory effect on the four kinds of bacteria in the experimental process. The bactericidal and bacteriostatic curves showed that the PC-CQDs have a good antibacterial activity against bacteria, with concentration-dependent antibacterial characteristics. Furthermore, they illustrated that P-CQDs hold excellent antibacterial effect towards Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria at a certain dose, with better ability towards Gram-positive bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on antibacterial mechanism of PC-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, S. aureus and E. coli were used as the representative Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria to study antibacterial mechanism of PC-CQDs, respectively. The surface charge of CQDs is an important factor in controlling its bactericidal activity through the adhesive process [69]. In the present study, negatively charged CQDs (NC-CQDs) with a zeta-potential of −12.77 mV were prepared and applied for the antibacterial mechanism discussion, especially for the adhesive comparison of CQDs on bacteria. Compared with PC-CQDs, NC-CQDs played weak antibacterial activity to variable bacteria, which was confirmed by the tested MIC larger than 500 μg/mL to all selected bacteria (data not shown). As shown in Fig. 3 A, the zeta-potentials of S. aureus and E. coli were −14.83 and −10.1 mV, confirming the negatively charged property. With the addition of 200 μg/mL of NC-CQDs, the zeta-potentials of the S. aureus and E. coli system were −17.03 and −16.97 mV, respectively, suggesting the lack of electrostatic interaction between NC-CQDs and bacteria. Upon the addition of PC-CQDs, the zeta-potentials of S. aureus or E. coli showed a significantly positive shift at 2.47 and 2.67 mV, respectively. These results suggested that S. aureus or E. coli adsorbed PC-CQDs via electrostatic interactions ascribed to the negatively charged teichoic acids on the membrane surface of S. aureus or the thin negatively charged cell membrane of E. coli [70]. Considering the internal fluorescence ability of PC-CQDs and NC-CQDs, confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was further applied to verify whether CQDs were attached to bacterial cells. As shown in Fig. 3B, the image of the interacting system of NC-CQDs and S. aureus had almost no fluorescence. Thus, the NC-CQDs hardly stained S. aureus due to the weak direct interaction. The bright field and merge images demonstrated that S. aureus was evenly dispersed, suggesting that the NC-CQDs did not affect the distribution of bacterial cells. A bright blue fluorescence image of the interaction system of PC-CQDs and S. aureus under 405 nm excitation was found, indicating that the PC-CQDs could effectively dye the bacterial cell through attachment. Furthermore, the bright field and merged images illustrated the aggregation of bacterial cells by the introduction of PC-CQDs, which may be due to their electrostatic attraction to each other [53]. The comparison of the CLSM of PC-CQDs and NC-CQDs on S. aureus supported that the PC-CQDs exhibited strong adsorption on the bacteria, while NC-CQDs played slight attachment on the bacterial cells. Combined with the zeta-potential results, these results revealed the significance of the surface positive charge of CQDs on the membrane-anchoring effect on the bacteria for antibacterial system fabrication. Moreover, considered the fact that the P-CQDs were prepared with introduction of PEPA in the reacting process, while NC-CQDs were prepared without PEPA. This result implied the significance of PEPA for the obtainment of antibacterial CQDs with surface positive charge.

The PC-CQDs exhibited stronger antibacterial efficiency on Gram-positive bacteria than on Gram-negative bacteria. The detailed mechanism of the differential antibacterial effects of PC-CQDs on different bacteria was explored. As shown in Fig. 3C, the cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria is mainly composed of peptidoglycan and phosphatidic acid (including leukophosphoric acid and membrane phosphatidic acid). The structure is relatively loose but thick. The cell wall of Gram-negative bacteria includes two layers of peptidoglycan and an outer membrane containing lipoprotein, lipid bilayer, and lipopolysaccharides [71]. The structure is intensive and thin. Although the PC-CQDs could be adsorbed on the bacterial cells via electrostatic interaction, the loose but thick cell membrane structure of Gram-positive bacteria was more appropriate to absorb PC-CQDs than that of Gram-negative bacteria with intensive and thin cell wall structure. In the present study, the real anchoring contents of the same dosage of PC-CQDs on the bacterial cells were investigated and compared using CLSM (Fig. 3D). The PC-CQDs could be attached to the cell bodies of the two types of bacteria. The fluorescence intensity of the PC-CQDs on S. aureus was significantly stronger than that on E. coli. Therefore, the attachment amount of PC-CQDs to the two species significantly differed; the attachment amount of S. aureus was much more than that of E. coli, indicating the different attach ability of PC-CQDs on variable bacteria with differential antibacterial effects of PC-CQDs on different bacteria.

The PI dye can only enter the structure (cell membrane)-damaged bacterial cells, stain the nucleus and then emit red fluorescence, while DAPI can pass through the intact membrane of cells, bind with DNA and then emit blue fluorescence. Thus, the live/dead bacterial staining was first carried out using DAPI/PI dye to evaluate the effect of PC-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , for untreated S. aureus, almost no dead cells were observed from the DAPI staining, while, almost all S. aureus cells were stained red from PI when treated with PC-CQDs. Most S. aureus cells died due to the rupture of the cell membrane. For E. coli, no dead cells were observed in the untreated group, but the PC-CQDs treated E. coli was counted less than that of S. aureus, which was about 40% of that of S. aureus that stained with PI.

Selected S. aureus, E. coli and MRSA as the model bacteria, the integrity of the bacterial cell membrane and the inclusion before and after the interaction with PC-CQDs were evaluated using TEM and SEM, as shown in Fig. 5 . The TEM images (Fig. 5A-a, b, c) of the untreated bacteria retained a well-defined morphology of a complete and smooth cell membrane. After the treatment of PC-CQDs, the cell walls and cell membranes became irregular, and the cell body disintegrated from the original structured inclusion with intracellular leak. Furthermore, SEM images (Fig. 5B-a, b, c) of untreated bacterial cells also showed clear edges and smooth walls. While, after incubated with PC-CQDs for 2 h, many membrane defects on bacterial cell surface combined with the presence of the leakage of intracellular contents was found from SEM images (Fig. 5B-d, e, f), suggesting that the bacteria was damaged and wrinkled. These results implied that the adsorption of PC-CQDs imposed physical or mechanical damage on the microbial cell wall or outer membrane to induce the overall physiological dysfunction of the bacteria and even the leakage of cytoplasmic components, ultimately leading to bacterial death [72,73]. Electrostatic interaction generally promotes PC-CQD interaction and entrance to bacteria. Such interaction process may not induce bacterial death, more action than the single binding effect played by PC-CQDs on the bacteria may be present to achieve broad-spectrum antibacterial ability even on AMR bacteria [23].

Positively charged quantum dots possibly interact or adsorb DNA or RNA through electrostatic attraction, nucleobase effect, and hydrophobic interaction [74], thus causing some errors during gene replication in bacterial growth [45]. In the present study, the possible absorption of gene on PC-CQDs was monitored using FAM labeled random sequence DNA (F-DNA), as shown in Fig. S6. F-DNA had a strong fluorescence emission at 520 nm, which was effectively quenched by PC-CQDs. This phenomenon implied that PC-CQDs could absorb and interact with DNA and then quench the labeled FAM [75]. Furthermore, CD spectroscopy of the interaction of DNA and PC-CQDs was performed. As shown in Fig. S7, a negative peak at 250 nm could be ascribed to the helicity of DNA, and a positive peak at 280 nm could be ascribed to the accumulation of bases [76,77]. With the gradient increase in PC-CQDs, the peaks at 250 nm and around 280 nm gradually changed and even turned the direction at high concentrations of PC-CQDs, indicating that the adsorption of PC-CQDs on DNA induced the conformational changes of DNA. This result inferred that the permeation of PC-CQDs into microorganisms may interact with DNA/RNA and lead to conformation alteration, which may eventually cause disturbance in bacterial cell function [45].

The possible generation of intracellular ROS of the interacting system of PC-CQDs and bacterial cell was evaluated. 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was applied as a ROS probe to detect the possibly produced ROS. In general, DCFH-DA has no fluorescence. It could freely enter the cell interior and hydrolyze to generate non-fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCFH) in the bacteria with the action of intracellular esterase. When ROS are produced in the cell, non-fluorescent DCFH could be oxidized to generate green-fluorescent DCF. As shown in Fig. S8, almost no fluorescence was observed in the interaction between DCFH-DA and S. aureus or E. coli. Similarly, the interaction of DCFH-DA and PC-CQDs also exhibited little no fluorescence centered at 525 nm. With the addition of PC-CQDs, the system of DCFH-DA and S. aureus or E. coli produced an obvious fluorescence at 525 nm with the different degrees, suggesting the generation of ROS in the antibacterial process [53]. Upon interaction with the same dosage of PC-CQDs, the same count of S. aureus generated stronger fluorescence at 525 nm than that of E. coli. It can be speculated that the same concentration of PC-CQDs incubated with S. aureus produced a larger amount of ROS, one of the reasons why PC-CQDs have different antibacterial effects on different bacteria. Furthermore, the production of ROS in the antibacterial process was investigated by CLSM using DCFH-DA as probe, as shown in Fig. S9. Under the excitation of 488 nm, PC-CQDs and DCFH-DA treated S. aureus or E. coli have bright green fluorescence in the dark field. While, the merge image exhibited that the blue fluorescence of PC-CQD with 405 nm exciting completely overlapped the green fluorescence from DCFH-DA probe. This overlapped fluorescence illustrated that PC-CQDs can attach and penetrate the bacteria, and then stimulate the bacterial cells to intercellular produce ROS.

The acquired drug resistance of bacteria is a potential drug safety hazard for drug development [5], thus the possible induced resistance of bacteria with long-term exposure to PC-CQDs was assessed. The serial incubation of the selected S. aureus, E. coli, and MRSA to PC-CQDs at sub-MIC over a 30-day period was carried out. The results revealed that the PC-CQDs exhibited retained antibacterial effects to the selected bacteria during the 30-day exposure without the production of detectable resistance (Fig. 6 A–C). PC-CQDs did not lead to bacterial resistance, it should be because the antibacterial mechanism of PC-CQDs is to contact the bacteria through electrostatic action and then enter the bacteria to stimulate the production of intracellular ROS. The interacting process made PC-CQDs would not bind specifically to some bacterial subunit completely. Such interacting behavior may not induce bacteria acquire AMR evolution [4]. The antibacterial property without detectable AMR evolution implied that PC-CQDs may be a promising and effective strategy for combating bacteria.

The effect of different concentrations of PC-CQDs on MC3T3-E1 cells at different interacting periods was measured using aCCK-8 kit to evaluate the in vitro cytotoxicity of PC-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 6D, the PC-CQDs with variable concentrations could retain the cells with >80% stability at 12 and 24 h, even the 500 μg/mL PC-CQDs acting on the MC3T3-E1 cells for 24 h, indicating the biocompatibility of PC-CQDs. CLSM for the intercellular ROS production of the interaction of PC-CQDs and cells was monitored to confirm the safety mechanism of PC-CQDs to cells. As shown in Fig. S10, when excited at 488 nm, the adherent MC3T3-E1 cells can be seen in the dark field emitting a blue fluorescent signal. However, the green fluorescence from the DCFH-DA probe was almost absent ascribed to the little ROS production, although PC-CQDs can stain the cells (Fig. S3). The weak ROS production in the system of PC-CQDs and cells may guarantee the safety of PC-CQDs to cells.

A possible hemolysis was assessed due to the functions of increased bacterial membrane permeability and reduced drug exudation of nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 6E, red blood cells were ruptured in sterilized double-distilled water but well dispersed in physiological saline containing different concentrations of PC-CQDs. After centrifugation was performed, the solution was almost colorless and transparent, and the red blood cells were deposited well on the bottom of the centrifuge tube with PC-CQDs. When the concentration of PC-CQDs was 500 μg/mL, the hemolysis rate was only 2.19% (<5%) compared to that in the negative control (Fig. 6F). Furthermore, the decreased hemolysis rate accompanied with decreased PC-CQDs content (2.19%–0.92%) indicated that PC-CQDs do not cause red blood cell rupture and hemolysis under normal physiological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing and safety of PC-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections, especially mixed bacterial infection, may hinder wound healing and even cause severe consequences. Herein, a multiplex bacteria (S. aureus and E. coli) infected skin excisional wound rat model was established to explore the in vivo antibacterial effect of PC-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 7 A, a full-thickness skin wound with a controlled diameter of 20 mm was scarlet. After the interaction with S. aureus and E. coli mixture for 48 h, the appearance of large area pustules suggested the successful infection on wound. After 3 days of treatment, the pustule area decreased and the scabs began to form in the experimental and positive groups, whereas the negative group still had obvious pus exudation on the wound surface. The pustules in the experimental and positive groups continuously decreased with extended treatment period accompanied by reduced wound areas. Compared with the still present exudates in the negative group on the 10th day, the wounds in the experimental and positive groups were significantly reduced with no exudates on the 7th day after treatment. After 14 days of treatment, the experimental and positive groups basically recovered with healed wound and new hair. By contrast, the wound in the negative group was still red and had leachate (Fig. 7B). The changes in the wound area from the different groups in the experimental process were compared. As shown in Fig. 7C, the wound areas were gradually decreased with the extended experiment period. All wound areas in every group before treatment were large with no significant difference. After treatment for 2 days, the shrinkage area of the wound surface in the positive and experimental groups was the largest, indicating the enhanced effect on promoting wound healing. From the 4th day to the last day of the experiment, the wound area in the negative group significantly differed (P < 0.01). However, no statistically significant difference was found between the positive group and the experimental group, indicating that the PC-CQDs had the same therapeutic effect as levofloxacin hydrochloride in the recovery of infected wounds.

The physiological and biochemical changes in the different rats were investigated. As shown in Fig. S11, the weights of the three groups appeared to be reduced after the initial infection and gradually increased after 2 days treatment. The weights of the rats in the experimental and positive control groups were higher than those of the rats in the negative group, suggesting the promotional effect of treatment for rehabilitation .Furthermore, the inflammation regression of the different groups were evaluated using the changed WBC and CRP. As shown in Fig. 8 A and B, the similar and normal ranges of the number of WBC and the content of CRP in the three groups before infection increased obviously after 48 h of infection. After 4 days of treatment, the WBC level of the experimental and positive groups were controlled and maintained, while that of the negative group continued to increase. With the continuous treatment, the WBC of the experimental and positive groups gradually decreased and achieved a normal WBC level before infection. On the contrary, the WBC level of the negative group remained high and significantly differed with the levels of the two treated groups and pre-infection. The CRP levels of the three groups gradually decreased, with more obvious decreased amplitude in the experimental and positive groups. The CRP level after treatment in the experimental and positive groups returned to the same level before the infection, whereas the negative group continued to have a high CRP level, which was consistent with the change regular of WBC. Meanwhile, the resudial bacterial colonies of the wound tissues from the three groups were compared (Fig. 8C). A large number of colonies were found in the negative group, but neither the experimental group nor the positive control group had obvious colonies. The above results indicated that the PC-CQDs have a similar efficacy to levofloxacin hydrochloride in the treatment of wounds and the killing effect on mixed bacteria. In addition, they could effectively reduce WBC and CRP in the infected rats and make inflammation subside, thus promoting the recovery of infected wounds. Furthermore, the antibacterial performance of several carbon dots (carbon quantum dots) was compared, as shown in Table S2. The comparison suggested that PC-CQDs exhibited the promising characteristics of broad-spectrum antibacterial ability with acceptable MIC and in vivo cure efficiency to mixed bacterial infection, and no evolution of AMR.

H&E staining of the wound tissue after 48 h of infection and 14 days of treatment was performed to assess the wound recovery. As shown in Fig. 8D, a large number of neutrophils appeared in the tissue at the infection site, which was consistent with the results of elevated blood WBC (Fig. 8A), CRP (Fig. 8A), and neutrophils (Fig. S12), suggesting that the wound was successfully infected by S. aureus and E. coli. In the infected wound tissues, thickened capillaries and erythrocyte aggregation in the vessels were found (shown by the blue arrows), suggesting the presence of edema in the wound. After 14 days of treatment, the neutrophils and erythrocyte aggregation almost disappeared in the wound tissues of the experimental and positive groups. Correspondingly, tight connective tissue and hair follicle tissue formed on the originally infected wound surface. For the negative group, the uncontrolled inflammation in the wound tissue was confirmed by the presence of numerous neutrophils, which was similar to the state of the initial infected wound. The pathological tissue results implied that the wounds treated with PC-CQDs healed well.

The further in vivo toxicity of PC-CQDs was evaluated on the basis of the in vivo healing effect of PC-CQDs. During the 14-day treatment period, all experimental rats had normal activities, diets, and mental states, and no symptoms, such as reduced diet and depressed spirit, were observed. After the treatment, the main organs of liver, spleen, lung, and kidney of the rats in the experimental group were euthanized and applied for safety investigation using H&E staining (Fig. S13). The pathological sections of the liver, spleen, lung, and kidney stained with H&E showed no significant difference in the tissue structure of the PC-CQDs group compared with that of the blank rats without any treatment, indicating that PC-CQDs did not cause pathological damage to the major organs. The results also implied that PC-CQDs have minimal toxic side effects on the main organs, or PC-CQDs could be converted into harmless substances in the body. Therefore, a preliminary conclusion could be that PC-CQDs are low in vivo toxicity nanomaterials for biomedical application.

In summary, a facile method was developed to prepare PC-CQDs as broad-spectrum antibacterial agent. The PC-CQDs exhibited effective antibacterial activity on common bacteria and even on drug-resistant bacteria due to the strong adhesive ability, interaction on gene, and generation of ROS with bacteria. They did not cause detectable evolution of further AMR, which was significant for developing a new kind of antibacterial agent. Based on the mixed bacteria-infected wound, the antibacterial ability and biocompatibility of the PC-CQDs could induce wound healing, with the comparative effect of levofloxacin hydrochloride but without any in vivo toxicity. Considering the simple preparation and safety of PC-CQDs, they have a promising potential to become an effective tool for severe bacterial infection in clinical medicine. Besides, the antibacterial mechanism of PC-CQDs could promote the consideration about the interaction and combating mode of new kinds of antibacterial agents.
